questions.question: Define a distributed system and explain its essential characteristics.
keyPoints: Collection of independent computers
Appears as single coherent system to users
Interconnected through communication network
Coordinates via message passing
Key characteristic: transparency

questions.question: Explain the concept of transparency in distributed systems. List and briefly describe the different types of transparency.
keyPoints: Transparency masks distribution from users
Types: Access, Location, Migration, Replication, Concurrency, Failure
Access: hides data representation differences
Location: hides physical location of resources
Migration: allows resource movement without user knowledge
Replication: hides existence of multiple copies
Concurrency: handles simultaneous access
Failure: masks failures and recovery

questions.question: What are the main advantages of distributed systems over centralized systems?
keyPoints: Resource sharing across nodes
Reliability/fault tolerance through redundancy
Scalability - can add more nodes
Openness - extensible architecture
Concurrent execution of tasks
Cost-effective - uses commodity hardware
Better performance for distributed workloads

questions.question: Discuss the key challenges or problems faced in designing and implementing distributed systems.
keyPoints: Heterogeneity - diverse components
Security concerns - more vulnerable points
Failure handling - partial failures
Scalability limitations
Concurrency control
Maintaining transparency
Network issues - latency, partitions
Lack of global clock for synchronization

questions.question: Differentiate between a distributed system and a computer network. Why is transparency considered the distinguishing factor?
keyPoints: Computer network: collection of interconnected computers maintaining individual identities
Distributed system: appears as single unified system
Transparency: masks distribution from users
In network, users know they're accessing remote resources
In distributed system, distribution is hidden
Message passing coordination vs direct resource access
Single coherent behavior vs independent operation

questions.question: What is heterogeneity in distributed systems? How does middleware help address heterogeneity?
keyPoints: Heterogeneity: diversity in hardware, OS, programming languages, software
System must handle this diversity transparently
Middleware: software layer between applications and OS/network
Provides uniform communication services
Hides underlying differences
Examples: ORB (Object Request Broker), RPC (Remote Procedure Call)
Enables interoperability between different systems

questions.question: Explain the concept of scalability in distributed systems. Discuss the differences between horizontal and vertical scalability.
keyPoints: Scalability: ability to handle growing workload by adding resources
Horizontal scalability: adding more nodes to the system
Vertical scalability: adding more power to existing nodes
Distributed systems primarily achieve horizontal scalability
Adds nodes without changing system structure
Challenges: coordination overhead, network latency
Scales geographically and logically

questions.question: Define distributed systems and explain how they achieve the 'single coherent system' appearance. Discuss the role of transparency, message passing, and middleware in achieving this goal.
keyPoints: Definition: collection of independent computers
Single coherent: unified view despite distribution
Transparency masks distribution from users
Types: access, location, migration, replication, concurrency, failure
Message passing: fundamental communication mechanism
Nodes coordinate by exchanging messages over network
Middleware: software layer providing services
Hides heterogeneity of underlying systems
Provides abstraction over network complexity
Enables location-independent access
Examples: RPC, message-oriented middleware
Together they create illusion of single system

questions.question: Discuss the fundamental challenges in distributed systems that arise due to their inherent characteristics. How do these challenges differ from those in centralized systems?
keyPoints: Partial failures: nodes can fail independently unlike total failure in centralized
Lack of global clock: synchronization challenges
Independent failures: network partitions, Byzantine failures
Heterogeneity: diverse components must work together
Security: more attack surfaces due to network exposure
Concurrency: multiple nodes accessing shared resources
Scalability: must maintain performance with growth
Openness: standard interfaces for extensibility
Transparency vs performance trade-offs
Unlike centralized: no single point of control, no shared memory
Complex debugging and testing
Distribution hidden from users but must be managed

questions.question: Explain the CAP theorem and its implications for distributed system design. How do real-world distributed systems make trade-offs between Consistency, Availability, and Partition tolerance?
keyPoints: CAP theorem: cannot simultaneously guarantee all three properties
Consistency: all nodes see same data at same time
Availability: every request receives a response
Partition tolerance: system continues despite network failures
Network partitions are inevitable in distributed systems
Must choose: CP (consistency + partition tolerance) or AP (availability + partition tolerance)
CP examples: distributed databases with strict consistency (ZooKeeper)
AP examples: Dynamo, Cassandra - eventual consistency
Trade-offs depend on application requirements
Modern systems often choose availability and partition tolerance
Use techniques: eventual consistency, quorum systems
Design must consider failure scenarios and business needs
